coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for rapid detection of glyphosate in miscellaneous beans
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Liyuan
Zhang, Ruiting
Wang, Jinchen
Chen, Jiayu
Yu, Runzhong
coredata.dc:description: To establish a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for the detection of glyphosate, nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. At an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the emission peak with maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained at 470 nm. The generation of 2.3-diaminophenazine by o-phenylenediamine oxidation produces a new fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm and a decrease in fluorescence at 470 nm. The fluorescence peaks at 470 and 570 nm form a ratio state. In the presence of glyphosate, reduces the oxidation of 2.3-diaminophenazine, this results in enhanced fluorescence at 470 nm and weakened fluorescence at 570 nm. Under the best conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system has good selectivity, the linear at glyphosate concentrations of 0.005–10 µg/mL, the limit of detection was 2.88 µg/kg, the limit of quantitation was 4.07 µg/kg. In addition, it is worth mentioning that this developed sensing system has shown good detection performance in 10 miscellaneous samples, the simple, efficient, and highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection sensing strategy is provided.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe modified with nitrogen and sulfur
Glyphosate
Rapid detection

Glyphosate detection based on ratiometric fluorescence sensing system
Detection of glyphosate in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Central Government for the Reform and Development of Local Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant number: 2020YQ16), the key research and development program guidance project of Heilongjiang province (Grant number: GZ20210071).

This work was supported by the Central Government for the Reform and Development of Local Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant number: 2020YQ16), the key research and development program guidance project of Heilongjiang province (Grant number: GZ20210071). This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors. Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) transmission electron microscopy (b) particle size distribution (c) fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of N.S-CQDs-NFP (d) salt resistance (e) acid and alkaline resistance (f) stability under sunlight conditions (g) stability under ultraviolet light.

Mechanism of the fluorescence sensing system (a) fluorescence spectra (b) UV-Vis spectra (c) excitation and emission spectra of N.S-CQDs-NFP, and UV-Vis spectra of 2.3-diaminophenazine (d) fluorescence lifetime.
Optimization of detection conditions (a) pH optimization (b) incubation temperature optimization (c) incubation time optimization.
(a) fluorescence intensity changes of F470/F570 corresponding to different concentrations of glyphosate (b) operational curve.
(a) selectivity (b) stability and (c) precision of the fluorescence sensing system for pesticides.
Determination principle of GLY based on ratiometric fluorescence sensing system.
Determination of GLY in spike samples with fluorescence sensing system.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: （The RSD is the standard deviation divided by the mean multiplied by 100%.）

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of GLY detection between ratiometric fluorescence sensing system and other fluorescence sensing systems.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (CdTe-CQD is an abbreviation for CdTe and Carbon quantum dot. DNA-AgNCs is an abbreviation for DNA-templated silver nanoclusters. N-SiQD is an abbreviation for N-Doped silicon quantum dots. G-CD is an abbreviation for green fluorescent carbon dots.)

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for rapid detection of glyphosate in miscellaneous beans

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information and Electrical Engineering, Heilongjiang Bayi Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To establish a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for the detection of glyphosate, nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. At an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the emission peak with maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained at 470 nm. The generation of 2.3-diaminophenazine by o-phenylenediamine oxidation produces a new fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm and a decrease in fluorescence at 470 nm. The fluorescence peaks at 470 and 570 nm form a ratio state. In the presence of glyphosate, reduces the oxidation of 2.3-diaminophenazine, this results in enhanced fluorescence at 470 nm and weakened fluorescence at 570 nm. Under the best conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system has good selectivity, the linear at glyphosate concentrations of 0.005–10 µg/mL, the limit of detection was 2.88 µg/kg, the limit of quantitation was 4.07 µg/kg. In addition, it is worth mentioning that this developed sensing system has shown good detection performance in 10 miscellaneous samples, the simple, efficient, and highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection sensing strategy is provided.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe modified with nitrogen and sulfur

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: China is a large agricultural country, the cultivated land area accounts for 7% of the world's cultivated land area (Larson, 2013). As the world's population continues to grow, ensuring food quality and safety is crucial (Umapathi et al., 2022). Contaminants in agricultural products include pesticides, pathogens, bacteria, fungi, etc. Pesticides are mainly associated with food borne illnesses. Pesticides are a class of chemical compounds, used to protect crops from pests, insects, weeds, bacteria and fungi (Lizeth et al., 2022). At the same time, the use of pesticides exceeds the safe limits for crops. Pesticides are mutagenic, carcinogenic and teratogenic, such as epithelial toxicity, hepatotoxicity and reproductive toxicity. Pesticides pollute soil, air, and water directly or indirectly, pesticides have harmful effects on human health. Organophosphorus pesticide is one of the common pesticides (Ajiboye et al., 2022). Pesticide contamination has been designated as one of the most worrisome global agricultural challenges.

Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are widely used in agricultural production (Yan et al., 2015). OPs is an acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitor. The function of AChE is to hydrolyze acetylcholine (ACh) to choline (Ch) and acetate (Liao et al., 2013). Therefore, the inhibition of AChE by OPs leads to the accumulation of ACh in the body, resulting in central and peripheral nervous system dysfunction (Wu et al., 2019). Therefore, it is urgent to establish a simple and rapid OPs detection method. Over the past few decades, a number of methods have been established to detect OPs, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Zhang et al., 2020), liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [Boneva et al., 2021), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Moinfar et al., 2021), etc. But these methods also have some drawbacks, such as relying on professional instruments and operators. To overcome these shortcomings, people are developing new ways to detect OPs. For example, enzymatic hydrolysis method (Zhou et al., 2021), colorimetric method (Lin et al., 2021), fluorescence method (Khaledian et al., 2021) and other methods. The fluorescence method has the advantages of rapid reaction, clear recognition signal, low cost and simple operation, it is favored by researchers in many directions (Alvandi et al., 2021). For example, Ghosh et al. used a one-step hydrothermal process to make carbon spots from erect marigolds. When exposed to chlorpyrifos, the fluorescence of this carbon spot is "turned off", but after exposure to benzenophos, the fluorescence of the carbon spot is "turned on". The quantitative detection of chlorpyrifos and benzenophos was successfully realized (Ghosh et al., 2021). Kashani et al. prepared a ratiometric fluorescent probe consisting of cadmium telluride quantum dots and carbon dots. In the presence of methyl parathion, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe quantum dots decreases, while that of carbon dots remains unchanged. The quantitative detection of methyl parathion was successfully realized (Fahimi-Kashani and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2020).

Carbon quantum dots have the advantages of simple preparation, good water solubility, low bio-toxicity, good bio-compatibility and unique physical and chemical properties. Carbon quantum dots have become the most attractive candidate materials for preparing photo-luminescence materials (Liu et al., 2020). Some studies have shown that the surface of carbon quantum dots is passivated and functionalized, for example, by doping with nitrogen and sulfur elements. The fluorescence quantum yield and the number of surface functional groups of carbon quantum dots can be increased (Liu et al., 2022). Doped carbon quantum dots have attracted much attention in optical fields such as chemical sensing (Sen et al., 2022) and medical diagnosis (Liang et al., 2022). According to most studies, when detected by a single fluorescent emission signal, the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots is easily affected by environmental factors, the re-producibility of single fluorescence intensity change is not high. However, one of the more popular methods is based on ratiometric fluorescence sensing systems, the fluorescence sensing system composed of two independent fluorescence emission wavelengths can self-calibrate, reduce the above limitations, effectively eliminate background interference, and significantly improve the sensitivity of the detection system.

In this work, corn stalks and p-aminobenzene sulfonic acid were used as raw materials. Nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe (N, S-CQDs-NFP) was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. A strategy for glyphosate (GLY) detection based on a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was developed. The method was applied to the detection of GLY in red bean samples, and the linear range, sensitivity and selectivity of the method was evaluated, and the detection mechanism was discussed. Finally, the method was used for the detection of GLY in 10 kinds of miscellaneous beans, which was enough to prove the feasibility of the method and the potential of practical application. Compared with conventional large-scale instrument detection methods, this method is inexpensive, simple, and does not require skilled operators. In addition, it does not suffer from the changes in enzyme activity and limited detection conditions of enzyme-linked methods, and is not subject to the high cost and complex procedures required for the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers. This shows great potential in the field of rapid detection.

The corn stalks are from local farmers (Daqing City, Heilongjiang Province), and peas, broad beans, green beans, black beans, soybeans, pinto beans, mung beans, rice bean, kidney beans and red beans are purchased from the agricultural market of Daqing City, Heilongjiang Province.

Sodium chloride, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, anhydrous p-aminobenzene sulfonic acid were analytically pure and provided by Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Copper sulphate and 2.3-diaminophenazine were supplied by Aladdin Ltd.
Glyphosate, methyl-parathion, fenthion, diazinon, chlorpyrifos were pharchased from Germany Dr. Ehrenstorfer Company.

200 high-speed pulverizer (Shanghai Yuan Wo Industry and Trade Co., Ltd.) and PHS.2 C precision pH meter (American Mettler Toledo Company) were used. AB204-N analytical balance (Shanghai Mettler Toledo Instrument Co., Ltd.) and RE-2000A vacuum rotatory evaporator (Ya Rong, Shanghai, China) were used.

Alpha1–2Ldplus Freeze Dryer (Germany Christ Company), KH-200 Hydrothermal Reactor (Gong Yi Ke Rui Instrument Co., Ltd.), NICOLETiS10 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), TU-1810 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer (Beijing Pu Xi General Instrument Co., Ltd.), RF-6000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and TEM-2100 F Transmission Electron Microscope (Jeol Ltd.), and FLS1000 Steady State/Transient Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of nitrogen-sulfur modified carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: After washing, drying and grinding, corn stalks powder were obtained through 80 mesh screening. A certain proportion of corn stalks powder with water was mixed， add anhydrous p-aminobenzene sulfonic acid and stir well. The mixture was transferred to a 200 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and reacted for 12 h at 270 ℃. After the reaction, the solution is cooled to 25 ℃. The light-yellow liquid was obtained after vacuum filtration. The obtained light-yellow liquid was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane, then the filtrate was moved into a 1 kDa dialysis bag and dialysis for 2 days to remove small molecules. The N, S-CQDs-NFP solution was obtained. Finally, poured the solution into a container and placed in the refrigerator.

The particle size of N, S-CQDs-NFP was measured by TEM-2100 F transmission electron microscopy. Firstly, the N, S-CQDs-NFP powder was sonicated with ultra-thin carbon film. Finally, the particle size was observed with transmission electron microscopy.

FTIR spectrum of N, S-CQDs-NFP was measured by using NICOLETiS10 fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The sample (1 mg) and dried KBr (100 mg) were ground into a uniform powder in an agate mortar, and then the sample was put into a thin plate and scanned in the sample chamber. The wave-number scanning range is 4000–400 cm−1. The resolution was 2 cm−1. The characteristic peaks of the samples were recorded, and each sample was determined in triplicate.

The peas, broad beans, green beans, black beans, soybeans, pinto beans, mung beans, rice bean, kidney beans and red beans samples were purchased from farmer’s markets in Daqing city of Heilongjiang Province (China, February, 2022) were ground into powder by using pulverizer and sifted through 80-mesh screens. The final spiked samples were 0.06, 0.03, 0.15, 6, 12, 18 and 30 µg of GLY added to 5 g of red bean samples, respectively. The fresh spiked samples containing GLY were prepared by spiking the stock solutions into miscellaneous beans samples, ensure the GLY to be well distributed, a reasonable amount of acetone and water were added to moisten the sample powders, stir carefully, then leave at 25 ℃ for 24 h. Pesticide samples are kept in sealed bottles and stored. (all experiments are in triplicate).

The samples were extracted by QuEChERs method. First vortex for 5 min, ultrasonic for 30 min, dissolve it thoroughly, centrifuge it at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was placed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube containing 450 mg PSA, vortexed for 1 min and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Transfer the supernatant to a chicken heart bottle, vacuum evaporation to nearly dry, melt back. Test with the above method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glyphosate detection based on ratiometric fluorescence sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal detection conditions, the process of GLY detection by ratiometric fluorescence sensing system is as follows. The GLY extracted from the samples, 100 µL GLY, 60 µL 500 µM Cu2+ and 60 µL 50 mM o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were mixed in PBS buffer (20 mM, pH = 7). After incubation at 40 ℃ for 5 min, add 250 µL N, S-CQDs-NFP solution. The final volume of the above mixture was set to 3 mL. The fluorescence intensity at 470 and 570 nm was measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The operational curve was obtained by plotting the relationship between F470/F570 and GLY concentration.

The important factors affecting fluorescence intensity, including pH value (4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10), reaction temperature (30, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 ℃), and reaction time (0, 1, 5, 10, and 15 min), were optimized in detail. For purpose of optimizing experiments and reducing errors, all experiments have to require in triplicate.

To evaluate the selectivity of this method, a series of pesticides with similar chemical structures to GLY, such as methyl-parathion, fenthion, diazinon, chlorpyrifos were used. According to the above optimal detection conditions, the added concentration of each pesticide is 500 µg/mL, and the added concentration of GLY is 240 µg/mL. And incubated at 40 ℃ for 5 min to ensure complete reaction. Fluorescence intensity (F470 and F570) were measured.

The stability was assessed by monitoring of the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs-NFP. The N, S-CQDs-NFP was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃, and the fluorescence intensity was measured every 3 days for 30 days. The change of fluorescence intensity was recorded to evaluate the stability of the detection method.

To evaluate the precision of N, S-CQDs-NFP sensing system, the difference of fluorescence response between different batches and different parallel samples of the same batch during the detection process was studied under the optimal detection conditions to evaluate the precision of the constructed sensing system in GLY detection.

Exploring the feasibility and practicability of fluorescence sensing systems. The peas, broad beans, green beans, black beans, soybeans, paprika beans, mung beans, rice bean, kidney beans and red adzuki beans were purchased from farmer’s markets in Daqing city of Heilongjiang Province. The samples were crushed and passed through an 80-mesh sieve. 5 g samples were weighed and added into a 50 mL centrifuge tube. Extracting was performed according to (2.4 Sample preparation). Fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrometer under optimal detection conditions.

As shown in Fig. 1N, S-CQDs-NFP has a spherical appearance and disperses well in aqueous solution. Fig. 1(b). N, S-CQDs-NFP particle size distribution was obtained by statistical analysis of particle size at 100 points. As can be seen from the bar chart, the particle size of N, S-CQDs-NFP ranges from 0.6 to 3 nm, with an average particle size of 1.57 nm.

The surface functional groups of N, S-CQDs-NFP were characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 1(c), a broad absorption peak observed at 3437 cm−1 corresponds to O-H, a strong absorption peak was observed at 2928 cm−1 belonging to C-H, another broad absorption peak observed at 2105 cm−1 belongs to S-H, the absorption peaks at 1623 and 1463 cm−1 are caused by the tensile vibration of CO and C-H bonds. The absorption peaks corresponding to stretching vibration of C-O and S-O bonds appear at 1183 and 1000 cm−1, respectively. The absorption peak at 588 cm−1 belongs to the tensile vibration of C-S bond.

The fluorescence stability of N, S-CQDs-NFP plays a decisive role in their successful application. The salt tolerance results of the N, S-CQDs-NFP showed that the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs-NFP remained relatively constant even though the ionic intensity of the NaCl varied to 1.0 M (Fig. 1d). The pH test showed that the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs-NFP was significantly affected when the pH changed at 2–12, but the fluorescence intensity remained relatively constant in the pH range of 2–9 and decreased with increasing pH in the pH range of 10–12 (Fig. 1e). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe did not decrease significantly after 12 h of white light irradiation (Fig. 1f), indicating that our N, S-CQDs-NFP solution has strong photostability. In addition, after continuous UV light excitation for 2 h, obvious lightless bleaching was observed (Fig. 1g). The results show that the N, S-CQDs-NFP has excellent fluorescence performance and good stability.

The N, S-CQDs-NFP with blue fluorescence was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with a yield of 18.65%, which is a green, environmentally friendly and low-cost new carbon nanomaterial. The prepared N, S-CQDs-NFP were comprehensively characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FTIR and fluorescence stability.

Several previous researches indicated that the carboxylate, amine, and phosphonate groups of GLY can reveal an extremely strong chelation effect to Cu2+ to form a Jahn-Teller distorted octahedron. The tridentate combination including carboxylate oxygen, one of the phosphonate oxygen atoms, and the monoprotonated secondary amine nitrogen atom chelates with Cu2+ to form two adjacent chelate rings oriented in the equatorial plane. Cu2+ locates at the center of a Jahn-Teller distorted octahedron. The last two axial positions are occupied by a phosphonate oxygen atom and a carboxylate oxygen atom from an adjacent GLY ligand. Consequently, it offers a superior possibility to establish a turn-on nanoprobe for GLY determination by integrating the N, S-CQDs-NFP with Cu2+ together (Wang et al., 2020).

In order to study the mechanism of the sensing system, fluorescence and UV-Vis spectroscopy were measured. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs-NFP showed a negligible decrease in Cu2+ or OPD, respectively. However, with the co-existence of Cu2+ and OPD, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs-NFP decreased significantly, and a new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 570 nm. It has been reported that Cu2+ oxidizes OPD to form oxOPD, and the solution changes from colorless to yellow, oxOPD has a strong absorption peak at 417 nm and a fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm. After adding GLY solution, the blue fluorescence at 470 nm was increased, and the fluorescence at 570 nm was decreased. In the presence of GLY, the absorption peak at 417 nm decreased significantly. This is due to the fact that GLY with carboxylate and phosphonate functional groups can chelate with Cu2+ to form a soluble N-(methyl phosphonyl) glycine copper (II) complex (Yang et al., 2022a, 2022b). Therefore, the added GLY interacts with Cu2+, hindering OPD oxidation, and a ratio state is formed after a new emission peak appears.

As shown in Fig. 2(b), the UV-Vis absorption spectra of several systems are shown. These include, N, S-CQDs-NFP and Cu2+ mixture, N, S-CQDs-NFP and OPD mixture, N, S-CQDs-NFP and Cu2+ and OPD mixture, Cu2+ and OPD mixture, OPD. Only one absorption intensity peak at 417 nm belongs to oxOPD. This indicates that no new complex is forming. Therefore, the possibility of electrostatic quenching is excluded.

To further investigate the detection mechanism. As shown in Fig. 2(c), at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, N, S-CQDs-NFP emits a strong blue fluorescence at 470 nm. Meanwhile, the maximum UV-Vis spectrum absorption peak of oxOPD was at 417 nm. The fluorescence excitation (black curve) and emission spectrum (red curve) of N, S-CQDs-NFP partially overlap with the UV-Vis spectrum (blue curve) of oxOPD. The oxOPD leads to fluorescence quenching of N, S-CQDs-NFP. So there's fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) between them. To further investigate the detection mechanism, fluorescence lifetime was measured to verify quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 2(d). The fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CQDs-NFP is 5.96 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CQDs-NFP, Cu2+, and OPD mixture was 5.12 ns. It indicates that the fluorescence lifetime path has changed. When GLY was added, the fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CQDs-NFP, Cu2+, OPD and Gly mixture was 5.38 ns. This shows that the addition of GLY interacts with Cu2+, hinders the oxidation of OPD and reduces the formation of oxOPD. The fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CQDs-NFP, Cu2+, OPD and GLY is lower than that of N, S-CQDs-NFP. However, the fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CQDs-NFP, Cu2+, OPD and GLY was higher than that of N, S-CQDs-NFP, Cu2+ and OPD. This means that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between N, S-CQDs-NFP and oxOPD is reduced in the presence of GLY.

To achieve high sensitivity of the sensing system, key experimental parameters including pH, incubation time, and temperature should be considered. As shown in Fig. 3(a), when pH increases, the emission intensity ratio (F470/F570) increases with pH value. As pH exceeds 7, the emission intensity ratio decreases. Therefore, pH = 7 was used throughout the experiment. For the incubation temperatures of N, S-CQDs-NFP, Cu2+ and OPD, the results are shown in Fig. 3(b), with the increase of incubation temperature, the emission intensity ratio (F470/F570) reached the maximum value when the incubation temperature was 40 ℃. With the increase of incubation temperature, the emission intensity ratio (F470/F570) decreased gradually. In subsequent experiments, the incubation temperature was 40 ℃. For the incubation time of N, S-CQDs-NFP, Cu2+ and OPD, the results are shown in Fig. 3(c). When the incubation time was 5 min, the emission intensity ratio (F470/F570) reached the maximum value. With the increase of incubation time, the emission intensity ratio (F470/F570) was stable. Therefore, 5 min was the incubation time for subsequent tests. In conclusion, the optimal reaction conditions were pH = 7, incubation temperature was 40 ℃, and incubation time was 5 min.

The response performance of the fluorescence sensing system to GLY was studied under the optimum experimental conditions. When the spiked sample is extracted, a range of concentrations of GLY are obtained. As shown in Fig. 4, with the increase of GLY concentration, the emission peak intensity at 470 nm increased, and the emission peak at 570 nm decreased. The fluorescence intensity ratio of F470/F570 increased with the increase of GLY concentration. In the range of 0.005–10 µg/mL, there was a linear relationship between GLY concentration and fluorescence intensity ratio of F470/F570, Y = 0.1015 X + 0.4896, R = 0.9971, the limit of detection (LOD) is 2.88 µg/kg (LOD = 3 × S/K, where S is the standard deviation. And K is the slope of the standard curve), and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) is 9.60 µg/kg (LOQ = 10 × S/K), each group of experiments was in triplicate.

To evaluate the selectivity of this fluorescence sensing system, a series of pesticides, chemically similar to GLY, such as, fenthion, methyl-parathion, diazinon, chlorpyrifos were detected under the optimal conditions mentioned above, and the fluorescence intensity were determined. The bar chart was drawn according to the F470/F570 fluorescence intensity ratio of the fluorescence sensing system. As shown in Fig. 5(a), only GLY had obvious effect on the enhancement of F470/F570 fluorescence intensity ratio, while other pesticides had almost no effect on F470/F570 fluorescence intensity ratio. Therefore, this method is highly selective for GLY detection.

In this work, the stability of the sensing system was evaluated by long-term monitoring of the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs-NFP. N, S-CQDs-NFP was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃, and the fluorescence intensity was measured every 3 days. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs-NFP remained 93.58% of the initial recorded fluorescence intensity after being stored for more than one month, indicating that N, S-CQDs-NFP had good stability as the signal output material of the fluorescence sensing system.

In order to verify the precision of N, S-CQDs-NFP sensing system, the difference of fluorescence response of the sensing system between different batches and different parallel samples of the same batch was studied under the optimal detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the maximum relative standard deviation (RSD) of the N, S-CQDs-NFP sensing system for the detection of GLY in ten different batches and five parallel samples of the same batch were 2.64 and 2.32%, respectively. The results showed that the N, S-CQDs-NFP fluorescence sensing system prepared in this paper had high precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Verify the feasibility of this fluorescence sensing system in real samples. By adding different amounts of GLY to 10 samples of peas, broad beans, green beans, black beans, soybeans, pinto beans, mung beans, rice beans, kidney beans and red beans. Ten samples were analyzed using the sensor system established in this paper. As shown in Table 1, the recoveries were 94.22–102.30% with RSDs lower than 3.29%. These results indicate that the fluorescence sensing system has a high recovery rate and high precision for the detection of GLY in actual samples, and can be used for the detection of GLY in beans. The RSD is the standard deviation divided by the mean multiplied by 100%.

Compared to other fluorescence methods reported ( Table 2), the proposed method shows relatively high sensitive. The detection process does not add any other reagents and enzymes, has the advantages of environmental protection, low cost, simple, good stability, high selectivity, easy to control the detection conditions and so on. This method provides a new idea for the rapid detection of GLY residues in beans.

As shown in Fig. 6, the maximum fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm was generated at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Cu2+ catalyzes the oxidation of OPD to 2.3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), oxOPD has an absorption peak at 417 nm. At the same time, a new fluorescence emission peak was observed at 570 nm. In addition, oxOPD significantly quenched the fluorescence at 470 nm due to FRET, showing a new fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm. In the presence of GLY, GLY functional groups can chelate with Cu2+ to form Cu2+-glyphosate complexes, prevents the oxidation of OPD, reduce oxOPD production, the fluorescence intensity at 470 nm increases, the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm decreased. With the increase of GLY concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 470 nm gradually increased, and the fluorescence at 570 nm gradually decreased.

The developed sensing system showed good detection performance in 10 kinds of miscellaneous beans, and the detection method had the advantages of simple preparation, low cost, good bio-compatibility, simple and rapid response, clear identification signal, self-calibration, and high sensitivity. At the same time, the limitation of this method is that the preparation conditions of N, S-CQDs-NFP are heated at 270 ℃ for 12 h, and the temperature is high, operational safety must be ensured. The N, S-CQDs-NFP should be stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃, otherwise, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs-NFP will be reduced. However, the preparation and storage conditions of N, S-CQDs-NFP can be ensured to achieve rapid and highly sensitive detection of GLY. The ratiometric fluorescence sensing system is accompanied by color change in the detection process, which can be further studied with the colorimetric signal as a probe for the detection of GLY, and the paper can be used as the substrate to prepare the color change test paper, and it can be linked to the smartphone APP to make the detection more convenient and intelligent. Of course, this detection technology can be used to detect GLY in water quality, vegetables, fruits, beverages, and living cells.

In summary, this work reports a preparation method of nitrogen-sulfur modified carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe with fluorescence luminescence properties. And based on nitrogen and sulfur modified carbon quantum dots nano-fluorescent probe and Cu2+ and OPD to form a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system. To achieve efficient, rapid and sensitive detection of GLY. At the same time, the detection of real samples is realized, indicating that this sensing system has potential in real sample detection.

Liyuan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jiayu Chen: Supervision, Resources. Runzhong Yu: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Jinchen Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Ruiting Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Central Government for the Reform and Development of Local Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant number: 2020YQ16), the key research and development program guidance project of Heilongjiang province (Grant number: GZ20210071).

ce:section.ce:para: This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
